title: Chapter 22
text: Chapter Nineteen R onin marched west. South was more lucrative — Colorado had been far more densely populated than Wyoming, before the Black Out — but it would take longer. There were places out here, sleeping in the hills, he hadn’t scavenged. The risk of returning to Cheyenne emptyhanded was higher, but time was moving against him now. Lara’s urgency had seeped into him. They needed to leave as soon as possible . Eighteen miles outside of town, he spotted a line of fence posts atop a hill, nearly lost in the scrub grass and dirt. As he climbed, his guidance system pushed him to turn east. To go back to Lara . She was in Cheyenne, waiting for him, trapped in the territory of the bots who killed her sister. Not because she wanted to be, but because he’d given her no other choice. All she’d asked was that he take her away from that place. And he forced her not only to stay, but to stay alone . All for a ring . They both knew it would trade for good credit. They both knew it would have been more than enough to acquire any supplies they needed, when combined with the trade from his last haul. Logic dictated that they should have taken it to the Market the moment she suggested selling it. They could have left together immediately afterward, without the agony of waiting, without suffering through uncertainty . It was an object only as valuable as the materials that made it — or it should have been. After her explanation of how humans used the rings, of what they were supposed to mean, after he’d seen the inscription on the inside of the band … Yours Until the End of Time . Simple words. Like the ring itself, they shouldn’t have held any meaning beneath the surface. But Ronin read them and knew he had to give it to her . He crested the hill, stopped beside one of the posts, and tapped it with the toe of his boot. The rotted wood broke with a dull snap , part of it disintegrating. The inhabitants of the old world had been fond of boundaries. Their reward was the boundless Dust . Ronin swept his optics over the area ahead. The ground ran down into a small valley, likely carved over millennia by a long-dried stream, before swinging back up another hill. The land continued like that for miles — peaks and valleys, steadily higher, steadily rockier. Sediment had gathered at the base of this valley over decades of countless storms. Rising from the accumulated dirt was a surprisingly intact, shingled roof . As he descended, his processors ran through the futile process of calculating the probability of him finding this particular place at this particular time. Before those numbers were completed, his attention shifted to Lara. How improbable was her existence ? Though there was science behind human breeding, most of it was left to chance. Lara’s ancestors, for untold generations, had come together and created offspring, passing on bits of their genetic code. Somehow, against near infinite possibilities, those couplings had resulted in her . And for Ronin to have walked past her shack, just as she was dancing, on a night when she had chosen to leave the door open a crack … The chain of unrelated occurrences leading to their meeting was staggering to contemplate . How much longer might Ronin have journeyed, unaware of the emptiness of his existence, if not for her? Would he ever have tasted the variety and depth of emotion she’d given him ? He circled the buried structure, unfastening the clasp on his holster and dropping his right hand to the pistol’s grip. Even if it couldn’t down most bots, the firearm packed enough of a punch to allow Ronin a chance to get in close . There were no visible signs of habitation; no trash, no path worn in the dirt near the only window above the ground line, no tracks of any sort. Ronin forced his active processing away from Lara — neither easy to do nor entirely possible — and changed his focus to his sensory inputs. The wind blew, as always, rustling the dry grass and kicking up loose bits of dust to patter on the roof . Ronin knelt in front of the window, pressing a palm to the frame. Only a few flakes of white paint remained, leaving the splintery gray wood exposed. Decades of abrasion and sunlight had left the glass too cloudy to see through . He applied upward pressure. The wood groaned, but the window didn’t move . After another optic sweep of his surroundings, he drew his pistol and used it to break the glass. The wooden cross that supported the four panes was brittle, snapping inward easily. He froze, listening, alert for any indication someone had heard him. The wind sighed its disapproval . What would Lara think of this? Would she be excited by the unknown, by the discovery ? Clearing out the remaining shards in the window frame, Ronin pulled himself inside feet-first. His optics adjusted to a lowlight setting as glass crunched under his boots. The room came into focus. Everything was coated in thick dust, but this had clearly been a bedroom. A rusted bedframe lay in one corner, and the skeleton of a mattress — more rotted wood, run through with corroded springs and draped in discolored, tattered cloth — stood against the wall . The furniture was falling apart, the walls crumbling to reveal the wooden ribs within them. He searched the room, floorboards creaking with every small shift of his weight, but found only a few bits of plastic amidst the dust and rat nests . He stepped through the door and into the hallway. Particles of dust, disturbed by Ronin’s passage, floated in the air — the only source of movement apart from him . Entering another bedroom, he continued his cautious search. There were the remnants of books in this one; their pages were yellowed with age, and crumbled at his touch. Gently, he brushed some of the dust off the cover of the book atop the bedside stand. It said DIARY . He couldn’t bring himself to pick it up . It was only more proof of what he already knew — people had lived here before the Black Out. They might have even survived it and remained for a time afterward. They’d slept in these beds, walked in the hallway, prepared meals in the kitchen he’d undoubtedly find before long. There was even the chance, infinitesimal but undeniable, that Lara had descended from this house’s former inhabitants . He moved to the closet. The sliding doors had fallen off their tracks, and lay on the floor nearby. Four moth-eaten garments hung amidst twenty-two empty clothes hangers — seventeen wire, five plastic. He holstered the pistol, placed his bag on the floor, and collected the hangers, bending the metal ones to reduce the amount of space they took up. Something on the shelf higher up caught Ronin’s attention when he was done . A cloud of dust accompanied the plastic bin as he pulled it down. Setting it on the floor, he crouched and carefully pried the lid open. Despite his caution, the brittle plastic snapped. He tossed the pieces aside . He reached into the bin and took out the stack of papers on top. Like everything else, they showed great age, but these did not disintegrate. Much of the paper was thick and textured, and they were in a variety of faded colors. There were shaky, simplistic drawings on many, and all of them had the same name on the back . Lindsey . It was always accompanied by a number, ranging from five to ten, and the drawings seemed to improve as the number increased . He placed the papers on the floor and removed the next object. It was small enough to hold in one hand, with a hard, plastic casing and five buttons along one side. There were no words on the buttons, only symbols — a square, a triangle pointing right, double triangles on two of the buttons, one set right-facing and the other left-facing. He pressed the button with the upward-pointing triangle . With an audible click, the front of the device — where there was a clear pane — opened. Inside was a small slot with two pegs. He had a sense that this had been old technology, even before the world fell apart, but the data as to its function was unavailable . He looked into the bin. Neatly arranged along the bottom were twenty-six plastic cases with words printed on their sides. MICHAEL JACKSON THRILLER, MADONNA BEDTIME STORIES, RATT REACH FOR THE SKY, DEF LEPPARD HYSTERIA, MEATLOAF BAT OUT OF HELL. There were numbers on all of them, but he couldn’t discern a meaningful pattern. Selecting one at random, he pulled it from its place and turned it . There was a picture on the front of a dark-haired woman holding a guitar. JOAN JETT Bad Reputation . Ronin ran his fingertips along the edge of the case, finding the grooves, and opened it. Another plastic object was inside, this one with two spools of tape visible inside. That’s what it was called — a tape. He removed it from the case and slid it into the tape player. It fit snugly, and the door closed without issue . Though he couldn’t be sure of it, he guessed that the tapes were recordings of music, at least in part. BEDTIME STORIES seemed to imply something else, but these were remnants of a bygone time. With his damaged memory, all he had were assumptions . Several seconds of fiddling with the device led him to the battery compartment, which was empty. That was good; leaking battery acid had destroyed the internals of many devices he’d discovered over the years. If he could figure out an alternate means of powering the tape player, Lara would have music to listen to. Maybe it would inspire her to move in new ways . He piled the bent hangers on the floor and transferred the tapes into his bag as neatly as possible. Then, he set the player atop them — along with the dusty ear pieces that seemed to go with it — followed by the hangers . Ronin stood and swung his pack over his shoulders, running his optics over the room one more time. They fell on the stack of drawings . Tabitha would continue to exist in Lara’s memory, but there was nothing to show for her existence apart from a simple grave just west of Cheyenne. She’d left nothing behind but her love for her adopted sister. Was that enough ? Crouching, he lifted the papers and returned them to the bin. Though it wouldn’t be much of a seal anymore, he replaced the pieces of the lid, lifted the bin, and slid it back onto the closet shelf. Perhaps they’d last a few years more than they would have on the floor. Perhaps Lindsey, whoever she’d been, would exist for a little longer because of the drawings . There was little else worth taking in the remainder of the house. Whether it had been scavengers or the former residents who’d picked it clean, Ronin would never know with any certainty. He found a pot in the kitchen that would clean up nicely, and a few more bits of metal and plastic that would pull in some credit . He stopped at the final door, on the far wall of the kitchen. The writing on its face was peeling off with the paint, but it was still legible . NOTHING FOR YOU HERE GO AWAY WE WILL FIGHT By the look of it, the words had been spray-painted there decades ago. No one had come or gone from this place in a long while, and, unless there was an active bot within, there’d be nobody left to fight . Ronin grasped the knob and pulled the door open . A narrow staircase led down, the wall giving way to the bare concrete of the building’s foundation. The ledge running beside the stairs was cluttered with objects made unidentifiable by the cobwebs and dirt that had gathered on them . He lowered his foot onto the first step and eased his weight onto it slowly, listening to the wood creak. When it didn’t break, he repeated the process with the next step. He’d fallen through floors and stairs before, and had never suffered more than minor damage, but it was best to take care. Especially with someone awaiting his return . Were these the kinds of places Lara wanted to see? The ruins of buildings once inhabited by forgotten people, telling stories no one could fully understand ? These places are her people’s history . Bots endured while humans were born, lived, and died, and Ronin could no longer decide who was more favored by the Creators. Were the long, dull lives of bots fuller than the quick, emotion-saturated lives of humans ? He shifted his focus back to his surroundings as he reached the solid floor of the basement. Overhead were the bare joists and beams from the floor above, the gaps dominated by thick, abandoned spider webs. Old blankets and sheets hung from the wood in most places, dividing the space. There was a stack of dilapidated boxes beneath the only visible window, which was completely buried in dirt on the outside . Ronin drew the handgun and reached forward with his empty hand, brushing aside one of the sheets. Dust rained from the fabric as he walked by. Grit crunched beneath his boots . His foot hit something, and the hollow, metallic sound of it was thunderous. Several clangs were followed by the noise of the object rolling on the concrete. He shifted his optics to follow the path in the dirt, ready to open fire. The can he’d kicked rocked slowly in place. There was more trash scattered around the floor — mostly cans and empty wrappers . Frowning, he moved forward, working his way through the maze of dangling fabric to the far wall. There were more boxes and plastic bins there, none in any better shape than what he’d seen upstairs. He turned to the left, through more sheets, and finally emerged in an open area . A workbench with a vise bolted near its center spanned the rear wall. More cans were scattered over its surface, along with small boxes, jars, and bottles. Articles of clothing, as dirty and worn as the bedding suspended from the rafters, hung along the edge of the bench as though placed there to dry . One item caught his attention, recognizable — despite the cobwebs enveloping it — because of the lever on one side. It was a press from a reloading kit . He shifted his optics to the floor, where a collection of blankets and pillows formed a pallet upon which three skeletons lay in ragged clothing. The two smaller ones were huddled in the arms of the third. Each skull had a small hole in its forehead . Several feet away, a fourth skeleton, taller than the rest, lay facedown on the concrete. The back of its skull was shattered, suggesting an exit wound . Ronin crouched beside the lone skeleton. Its fingers were wrapped around a rusted pistol, one arm twisted awkwardly beneath its torso. In its other hand, it held a paper wrapped in a plastic sheath. Ronin slid it out of the claw-like grip. The writing on the paper was in a sloppy scrawl, not unlike the latter entries in the journal Lara found in the attic . It was mercy. There was no other choice, and it was a mercy to do it. You’re not going to have us again, even after we’re dead. Not going to have us or ours! You made me kill them. Made me It was a mercy A MERCY Lord, have mercy on me . Folding the paper, Ronin stared at the remains. He’d spent many years longing for the return of his memories from the before time, had thought himself incomplete without them. But were these the sorts of memories stored in the corrupted part of his mind? Was the data full of scenes like this ? He lifted the hand and replaced the note. His optics flicked to the pallet; which of the smaller skeletons had been Lindsey ? Having lost his desire to search the basement further, he rose and walked to the workbench. The reloading press would likely trade for a good amount, but it would have to be enough regardless. He needed to get back to Lara . The bolts fastening the press to the benchtop were rusted, and groaned in protest when he clamped his pliers onto them and exerted pressure. For all that, they gave way easily. Within less than a minute, he had the press in his bag, sweeping in the nearby aluminum cans atop it . Lara would be pleased. He’d asked for a few days, but if he started back now, he’d be back in Cheyenne within twelve hours of leaving . He walked through the blankets, moving them aside with an outstretched arm. The air filled with displaced dust, obscuring his vision. Something caught at his ankle. It was thin, wire-like, and bent slightly. There was a short, metallic scrape, followed by the sound of another piece of metal falling onto the concrete . The fraction of a second Ronin had to anticipate what was coming proved too little. His processors fired off a volley of immediate commands . Leap away . Turn around . Shield optics and torso . Drop to the floor . The blast hit him before any of those actions were performed. His optics flickered to static, and his audio receptors took a burst of one hundred and eighty decibels before cutting out. His dermal sensors registered the impact first, a rush of hot air and then bits of shrapnel tearing his skin to ribbons. Sharp pain blazed through him. The sheets and blankets around him caught fire, falling atop him as he stumbled backward. His right leg locked, nearly toppling him over . He registered the intense heat both as a measurement of temperature and as searing agony that melted the electrodes in and beneath his skin . The haul . Whatever damage he had taken — was taking — they wouldn’t be prepared to leave Cheyenne if he didn’t bring in more salvage . Lifting both hands, he fumbled through the mess of burning fabric and melting synthetic flesh to tear the straps of his rucksack and thrust it away. His optics were chaotic, and he didn’t know if it was because of the fire or if they’d suffered more serious damage. Grasping whatever fabric he could, he pulled off the blanket and his burning coat, thrusting them away. He slid his foot back to put distance between himself and the blazing cloth . His heel caught on something — probably his discarded pack — and it was enough to disrupt his delicate balance. Ronin fell backwards. He was aware of the heavy thunk of his head hitting the concrete, and the majority of his systems went into standby . They had a trap set . You’re not going to have us, even after we’re dead . Diagnostics booted, assessing the damage . External temperature normalized. Casing penetrated in thirteen locations. Severe damage to synthetic epidermis, sixty-two percent loss. Power cells stable, 86.65 percent charge remaining . One by one, he powered his systems on . Motor functions impaired by damage to right knee joint. Audio receptors functioning normally. Left optical input offline . The joisted ceiling flickered back into view through a smoky haze. Static crackled across the feed, and, for several seconds, white bars scrolled through the image. He brought a hand up. The charred cuff of his coat was still around his wrist, but most of his skin was gone. A triangular piece of shrapnel jutted from his forearm. He pinched it between two fingers and worked it loose . His system of dermal sensors was the last to reboot. It began with a single, small surge. Within an instant, it burst into waves of searing electric that locked his limbs and spread agony through his system. He disabled the interface, cutting off all epidermal sensation. The damaged network was causing an overload . The dust particles in the air slowly settled, falling upon Ronin like they had over everything else in the house. He’d always acknowledged the strong possibility that his end would come like this. Alone in a forgotten place, a ruin…deactivated to become another artifact of a bygone era. The traders in whatever town he’d most recently based himself in might wonder where he’d gone, but he wouldn’t warrant a second thought . Dustwalkers came and went, and, usually, never came back again . But there was someone who would give him a second thought. There was someone waiting for him, someone depending upon him. He’d given his word; he would return to her. Not because he was fond of Cheyenne, not because his residence held any importance for him. Not because he needed the trade . Ronin needed Lara . He rolled onto his side and attempted to regain his feet. His right knee refused to bend, its function inhibited by embedded shrapnel. Reaching back, he wrapped the broken straps of his pack around his hand and dragged it closer. He moved the surviving tools from his belt into his bag, dropped onto his belly, and crawled toward the stairs .
